title: Making a portfolio cover, a flat-spine folder for loose sheets
subtitle: A cloth-covered folder that keeps large loose sheets together
excerpt: A handmade portfolio is two rigid boards joined at the spine by a cloth hinge. It protects loose prints, artwork, or documents without binding them, the sheets stay flat and removable inside. This version uses a simple turn-in cloth cover on 3 mm boards.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapter on portfolios and slip-cases: the turn-in cloth construction is described as the simplest protective folder structure in common use.

glossaryTerms.definition: The portion of covering material folded over the edge of the board and glued to the inside face.
The measured space between the two boards at the spine; it must accommodate the intended contents without forcing the boards apart.

content.text: A portfolio is the simplest protective structure in bookbinding. No sewing, no text block, no endpapers, just two boards covered in cloth and hinged at the spine by a strip of the same cloth. The making takes an afternoon and requires little more than clean cutting and patient gluing.

attrs.title: Cutting tool safety
attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones, blunt blades skid. Change the craft-knife blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut against a metal ruler, never against a plastic or wood edge. Keep the non-cutting hand at least two finger-widths behind the blade's path.

attrs.title: Bookbinding adhesives
attrs.body: PVA and wheat-paste are safe for indoor use without special ventilation. Clean brushes with warm water immediately after use.

items.name: Bookbinding board, 3 mm
items.notes: Two pieces cut to the size of the contents plus 5 mm on three sides (head, tail, foredge). For A3 prints, the boards are approximately 430 × 315 mm.

items.name: Book cloth
items.notes: One piece approximately 100 mm larger in all directions than the two boards combined. Unbleached or coloured; the cloth must be pre-backed to prevent PVA soaking through.

items.name: PVA, acid-free
items.notes: The adhesive for the cloth-to-board bond and the turn-ins.

items.name: Paste brush (50 mm flat)
items.notes: Apply PVA evenly in all directions to avoid ridges.

items.name: Craft knife and cutting mat, A3
Pencil
items.notes: For marking board positions on the cloth.

content.text: Measure the full thickness of the contents and add 2 mm. This is the
, the space between the two boards at the hinge. Mark the board positions on the cloth with pencil: front board, gap, back board, with 15 mm of cloth extending on all four outer edges for the

Apply an even coat of PVA to the outer face of the front board. Place it on the cloth, aligning with the pencil marks. Rub down firmly through a sheet of scrap paper, working from the centre outward to push out any air. Repeat for the back board. Allow two minutes for the adhesive to grab before turning the cloth over.

Cut each corner of the cloth at 45°, leaving a gap of roughly 1.5 times the board thickness from the board corner, this prevents the corner cloth from bunching. Apply PVA to the turn-in strips and fold them over the board edges one side at a time. Work the long sides first, then the short sides. Use the bone folder to press the cloth firmly into the edge and into the corners. A damp cloth held over a lumpy corner for ten seconds softens the cloth for cleaner pressing.

Cut two pieces of cartridge paper or thin decorative paper to fit the inside of each board, 3 mm smaller on all sides than the board. Apply PVA to the paper and press into place. The liner paper hides the turn-in margins and gives the portfolio a clean interior.

Close the portfolio and press it flat under a board and a stack of heavy books. Allow at least two hours before handling, ideally overnight. PVA pulls the board as it dries, pressing prevents warping. If the board has still warped slightly after drying, dampen the inside lightly with a sponge and press again.

A ribbon tie at the foredge, a length of grosgrain or linen ribbon glued under the liner paper on each board, keeps the portfolio closed in transit. For a more archival version, use Japanese tissue as the hinge material and acid-free board throughout.
